title: Stackable component wrap + layout reflow anomalies on Safari/Firefox mobile — aggregation and prioritized QA
preview:
Problem statement:
Multiple regression reports from QA and customer support indicate that certain 'stackable' components (horizontal token stacks, responsive button groups, compact table rows, and chart legend chips) unexpectedly overflow, reflow, or collapse into a single-line on constrained viewports when used in Safari on iOS and Firefox on Android. The behavior is inconsistent across breakpoints and sometimes linked to momentum scrolling or delayed font fallback. This ticket will centralize findings, record reliable repro steps, propose short-term mitigations, and create engineering tickets for long-term fixes.

Scope:
- Reproduce and classify failures into deterministic and timing-dependent categories.
- Produce minimal test cases (static HTML + component instances) for each category.
- Ship a short-lived CSS patch and a token spacing tweak for the next minor UI Kit release to reduce customer impact.
- Create ENG follow-ups for runtime fixes (layout APIs, prefer-reduced-motion interactions, kerning-aware token truncation).

Non-goals:
- Full refactor of responsive grid system (out of scope for this sprint).

title: Model Lineage Traffic Steward — compact wireflow for multi-model routing and rollback
preview: Goal: Prototype a compact rollout composer focused on multi-model traffic steering and per-variant lineage inspection. The flow should let engineers and PMs direct traffic across model variants (e.g., quantized / float32 / distilled), create conditional routing rules by request attributes, preview model lineage for sampled requests, and execute fast rollback at cohort or model-variant granularity. Emphasis: clear foreground actions for emergency snapback, explainable traces to audit which model served which sample, and a simulation mode that forecasts metric drift (latency, cost/unit, regression rate) before applying weights.

title: Incident Worksheet: Structured Error Triage & Retry Heuristics for Console
preview: Design and prototype an Incident Worksheet canvas inside the Redwood Console that guides engineers through structured error triage for failed inference requests. The worksheet synthesizes telemetry, payload diffs, KV/cache hints, model variant fallbacks, and retry heuristics into a concise, actionable UI. Goal is to reduce mean time to resolution (MTTR) for failed request classes and make rollout rollback decisions (canary vs full) more confident. Target personas: SRE/ML Engineer on-call, Platform engineer running Dedicated clusters, and PM owning SLAs. Key screens: 1) Headline summary with failure taxonomy and cost delta; 2) Timeline + sequence map of retries & fallbacks; 3) Payload diff & ephemeral KV snapshot with redaction controls; 4) Retry rules visualizer with suggested playbook actions (backoff adjustments, model variant fallback, temporary routing); 5) Quick actions panel including replay, rerun with altered params, create rollback rollout and attach to incident. This ticket covers initial hi-fi prototype, a 3-user moderated usability test, and a short design review with Eng/PM for feasibility and data needs.

title: GPU Hub Minimap & Autoscale Gestalt — compact controls and triage overlay
preview:
Goal: Design a compact, high-density dashboard module (GPU Hub Minimap) for the Dedicated Capacity console that surfaces pool health, autoscaling posture, and rapid triage controls in a single glance. The module must work as a tiled card in the fleet overview and as a focused overlay for incident response.

Why this exists: Customers operating Dedicated GPU pools need a fast, low-noise way to detect emergent latency/cost regressions and take immediate mitigations (burst enable, drain, fallback route) without switching pages. Current fleet pages are too verbose for on-call flow; this ticket explores a condensed gestalt that preserves actionability.

Primary users: SREs, infra/platform engineers, customer success who perform triage or quick tuning on Dedicated pools.

Design constraints and guiding principles:
- Compactness: fit key signals into a 320x200 card while remaining readable on 1440px+ consoles.
- Action-first: expose 3 immediate actions (burst toggle, scale-to-min/max presets, route-fallback) with confirmations and keyboard shortcuts.
- Signal clarity: show health (green/amber/red), 1h and 24h sparkline for latency and token throughput, and a micro heatmap for request distribution.
- Cost-awareness: include ephemeral cost delta estimate for burst actions (e.g., "+$12 / hr") and token-level cost badge.
- Accessibility: color + pattern encoding for health states, focusable controls, and ARIA labels for screen readers.
- Progressive disclosure: detailed metrics and logs open into full Fleet Insights page or runbook links.

Deliverables for this ticket:
- Low-fidelity wireframes for Hub Minimap (tile + overlay) and two variants of the autoscale control (slider vs. segmented presets).
- High-fidelity prototype in Figma showing micro-interactions: burst ramp animation, quick-confirm modal, and cost delta tooltip.
- Clickable scenario flows for: normal ops glance, on-call triage (latency spike), and scheduled burst for heavy batch jobs.
- Usability test script (5 participants) and success metrics.
- Handoff package: tokens, spacing, component spec, copy guidelines, and a short accessibility checklist.

Acceptance criteria:
1) Designer prototype demonstrates all actions and shows cost delta for burst operations.
2) Usability test: 80% of participants complete triage scenario within 90s without assistance.
3) Frontend dev sign-off on component feasibility (max 2 small behavior compromises).
4) Product approves copy and fallback behavior (canary fallback route must be reversible).

Design notes / non-goals:
- This ticket intentionally scopes out rewriting the whole Fleet Insights page — only a compact card + overlay are in scope.
- Deep perf telemetry correlation (trace waterfall) will link to a separate ENG ticket; we will surface summary metrics only.

Links to artifacts:
- Figma (prototype v1): https://www.figma.com/file/REDWOOD-DEDICATED/GPU-Hub-Minimap-v1
- Confluence PRD: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/UX/pages/4367/GPU+Hub+Minimap+PRD
- GitHub stub for frontend hooks: https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/4821
- Runbook reference: https://drive.redwood.com/drive/folders/ops-runbooks/dedicated-pools

title: Secure Edge: first-run network canvas & CLI-first install visual assets
preview:
Goal: Produce a cohesive set of onboarding visuals and product screenshots that support a CLI-first private deployment experience for Secure Edge (Redwood Private). This ticket covers: a network canvas storyboard for the operator flow, high-fidelity screenshots for the command-line installer and post-install Console snapshots, an upgrade/rollback visual pattern for air-gapped appliance upgrades, and developer-facing microcopy for each step.

Scope and deliverables: see `assets` list. Visual direction: minimal, operator-focused, emphasize connectivity state and safe-fallback affordances. Illustrate network topology as editable nodes (VPN, NAT, peering) rather than static diagrams so engineers can map their infra during first-run. Avoid marketing-style hero images; favor contextual screenshots + annotated overlays for docs.

Out-of-scope: marketing hero visuals, full localization deliverables (we will provide snapshot-ready text for i18n later), alternate color themes.

Links: Figma prototype (concept + screens): https://www.figma.com/file/secure-edge/onboard-cli-canvas (private); Confluence brief: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/OPS/Secure+Edge+first-run+brief; example GH PR with installer prompts: https://github.com/redwood-inference/private-installer/pull/271

Design constraints and notes: - Screens must be clear at 1200px content width for docs pages and 1440px for Console mockups. - Provide both dark and light background variants for screenshots used in docs. - Ensure terminal screenshots have selectable text overlays (PNG with separate caption file) so docs can copy commands easily. - Upgrade UI mockups must show version diff, preflight checks, estimated downtime, and rollback CTA. - Include a micro-interaction spec (120ms fade for connectivity state transitions, 200ms slide for node expand).

Acceptance criteria: - Delivered Figma file with named frames for each asset in `assets`. - 12 high-res PNG/JPG export images (1200–1440px wide) plus two animated GIFs: one short loop of network canvas interactions, one of the upgrade modal flow. - Copy approved by PM and Engineering (see comments). - Hand-off package in Drive with export pipeline and screenshot naming guide.

title: Miniature Request Lens: compact span timeline & correlation scroller
preview:
Goal: Design a compact, embeddable 'Miniature Request Lens' that fits into the Console request list and rollout detail panels for fast triage of problematic requests without navigating to the full trace inspector. The component should surface the most actionable information (critical spans, latency hotspots, correlation IDs, related traces) while preserving alignment with the Console's visual language and accessibility standards.

User problems we solve:
- Engineers and on-call SREs need a lightweight view to triage slow requests inline (list rows, rollout preview) without waiting for the heavy trace canvas to load.
- Product managers want a concise representation to compare pre/post-rollout performance in A/B rows.
- Designers need a consistent compact UI pattern to reuse across dashboards, alerts, and the rollout audit stream.

Design constraints and principles:
- Minimum footprint: target height 136px when collapsed in a table row and up to 320px when expanded in-place.
- Progressive disclosure: show a single horizontal micro-timeline with a density heatline, primary span chips (max 5), and a correlation ID affordance. Expand reveals timeline scrubber and a 'jump to full trace' action.
- Low cognitive load: color usage limited to status (ok/warn/err) and a neutral density palette for non-highlighted spans.
- Performance: SVG-based sparkline + request-level caching so the mini lens renders in < 120ms for typical list pages.
- Accessibility: keyboard focusable scrubber, ARIA labels for span chips, and contrast ratios per WCAG 2.1.

Proposed components (atomic breakdown):
- Micro-timeline sparkline (SVG) showing relative timing, shaded prefixes for cache hits and KV lookups.
- Span chips (compact pill) each with an icon for span type (http/db/kv), latency in ms, and hovercard with a 1-line operation name.
- Correlation scroller: a horizontal scroll area that shows related correlation IDs and their brief status (succeeded/errored/slow) so users can open sibling requests.
- Minimap scrubber: tappable scrub handle that updates a tooltip with absolute timestamps and span focus.
- Quick actions bar: hover-reveal actions for 'Open full trace', 'Pin to rollouts diff', and 'Create incident' (with prefilled fields).

Prototype notes and interactive flows:
- Prototype built in Figma (link below) with two main flows: (1) inline expand from request list, (2) view embedded inside rollout comparison row.
- Interaction: single-click expand, Ctrl+click opens full trace in new tab; scrub updates inline span highlight and triggers a small telemetry call to prefetch the trace payload.
- Mobile behavior: collapse to a single-line summary with the correlation badge and a chevron to full-screen mini-lens modal.

Design trade-offs discussed:
- Density vs. readability: we capped visible span chips at 5 and use a +N overflow indicator to avoid cramming. Some engineers asked for an option to switch to a 'dense mode' for debug sessions; deferred to follow-up.
- Inline caching: prefetching improves perceived speed but may lead to extra network calls; platform telemetry team requested a feature flag to gate aggressive prefetch.
- Visual fidelity vs. implementation cost: proposed SVG sparkline requires runtime sampling of span timings; ENG suggested a fallback static PNG for older browsers.

Feedback and decisions from reviews:
- 2026-02-25 (PM review - Lucia Ortega): "Love the correlation scroller for rollout comparisons. Ensure the quick actions map to the existing rollout audit events schema." -> Added dependency PM-903.
- 2026-02-28 (Engineering review - Rahul Patel): "Prefetch should be opt-in. We'll expose a prefetch config via infra and rely on KV cache headers to avoid extra work." -> Marked dependency ENG-48200 and added performance acceptance criterion.
- 2026-03-03 (Design critique - Nora Kim): "Chip labels need better truncation rules and accessible names. Use explicit ARIA labels with operation + latency." -> Implemented ARIA guidance.

Acceptance criteria (for handoff):
- Figma prototype with interactive scrub and hovercards available and linked; design token mapping completed.
- Edge-case states: empty spans, partial traces, and high-cardinality correlation lists rendered gracefully.
- Performance budget: first-paint of mini-lens <= 120ms on internal staging dataset.
- QA checklist: keyboard navigation, screen reader verification, and visual regression tests.

Implementation notes for engineers:
- Suggested data shape: request_summary { request_id, start_ts, end_ts, top_spans:[{op,type,duration_ms,status}], correlation_ids:[{id,status}], health_status } (note: this is guidance for API contract; final shape to be agreed with ENG).
- Prefetch flag name: tracing.mini_lens_prefetch (boolean).
- Telemetry events: mini_lens_open, mini_lens_scrub, mini_lens_action_click with minimal payload for privacy.

Artifacts and links:
- Figma prototype: https://www.figma.com/file/REDACTED/mini-lens-proto-2026
- Confluence spec: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/CONSOLE/pages/REDACTED/Miniature+Request+Lens+Spec
- Dev PR (placeholder): https://github.com/redwood-inference/console/pull/REDACTED
- Usability test notes: https://docs.google.com/document/d/REDACTED

Next steps:
- Resolve ENG-48200 on prefetch gating and finalize API contract (owner: Rahul Patel).
- Implement first-pass component in staging and run performance smoke test (owner: Marco Alvarez).
- Usability round with on-call engineers and PMs; iterate on chip truncation and action discoverability (owner: Ava Chen).

title: MiniCoach — inline micro-actions for cost literacy during rollout setup
preview: Problem: users configuring model rollouts and capacity commits often lack quick, contextual guidance about the immediate cost impact of toggles (batching, KV cache, quantization). Large doc-driven onboarding interrupts flow. Goal: design a compact, dismissible "MiniCoach" micro-overlay that surfaces a single actionable tip tied to the control the user is interacting with, with a one-tap action (apply suggestion) and a link to deeper docs. The component should be small, non-modal, accessible, localizable, and trackable for analytics.

title: Audit Lens: operator access mapping & temporal grant explorer prototype
preview: Goal: Design a compact, searchable Audit Lens component for the Redwood Console that surfaces who has access to a resource, why they have it (role, group, direct grant, inherited), and how long that access lasts. Scope includes: workspace/org role mapping, temporal grants visualization, one-click access simulation, and an exportable incident snapshot for audits. This ticket covers the low-fidelity to interactive prototype, engineering handoff artifacts, and a first round of moderated usability tests with platform engineers and security reviewers.

title: Glassweight token harmonization for mixed-brand consoles and high-density table visuals in low-light mode
preview:
Background: Multiple brand integrations and a recent push toward translucent "glass" surfaces have exposed inconsistencies in dark-mode token mappings. High-density table rows, compact controls, and chart stroke/area visibility suffer when brand accent intensity collides with contrast guardrails. This ticket harmonizes a small, pragmatic token set ("glassweight" family) and defines component-level overrides so partners can opt into brand intensity without breaking accessibility or telemetry dashboards.

Goals:
- Define a concise glassweight token family for dark themes that controls surface translucency, tint strength, blur/opacity trade-offs, and micro-elevation for compact components.
- Map existing brand-accent tokens to conservative dark-mode fallbacks with quantized strength levels (low/medium/high) for predictable behavior.
- Introduce per-component override rules for tables, charts, and compact buttons so visual density and legibility are preserved.
- Provide a migration guide and automated codemod to update console components to the new tokens.

Non-goals:
- Do not change core brand color palettes themselves. This work is about mapping and overrides for the console surface in dark mode only.
- Not addressing mobile platform-specific rendering optimizations (separate ticket).

Success metrics:
- No critical contrast regressions in our eval suite (contrast ratio >= 3.0 for chart lines and >= 4.5 for primary button text in dense table rows).
- A/B test on 5 internal dashboards shows <= 0.5% increase in page render time attributable to visual style changes (target: negligible perf regression).
- Rollout plan accepted and togglable per-tenant in Dedicated/Private deployments.

title: Tokenwise requant budgeter and kernel provenance harness for dovetailed attention scheduling
preview:
Problem: Mixed-precision pipelines (INT8/FP8) introduce per-token dequant/requant overhead that is disproportionately paid during prefill and early decode windows, increasing P95 latency and token cost for short-turn conversations. Current kernel selection lacks fine-grained token-aware budgeting and provenance telemetry, making it hard to decide when to incur a dequant vs. pick a slower FP32/FP16 kernel.

Proposal: Implement a "tokenwise requant budgeter" in the runtime scheduler that tracks a sliding budget across the active token window and decides, per token/head-group, whether to route to an INT8 fastpath with a requantization at the epilogue or to a safer FP8/FP16 kernel. Complement this with a "kernel provenance harness": lightweight telemetry attached to each kernel decision that records the features used to decide the path (sequence length, kv-hit ratio, headwise entropy, batch shape, latency budget remaining, cache hits) and the observed cost/latency after execution.

Core deliverables:
- Scheduler change that maintains a per-request token budget (configurable by route/model) and makes tokenwise routing decisions during prefill and decode. Budgeting policy should support: fixed token budget, sliding-window proportional budget, and conservative holdback for canary traffic.
- Kernel provenance harness: a compact event emitted per token-chunk with labels (kernel_variant, policy_decision, reason_codes). Events routed to an internal perf stream for post-hoc analysis and automatic policy tuning.
- Dovetailed attention staging: schedule dequant/requant epilogues to overlap with subsequent attention projection compute when safe, reducing visible tail latency.
- Simulation tool: a batch-mode simulator that ingests historical traces (Confluence link below) and runs candidate budget policies to estimate delta cost and latency distribution before deployment.

Acceptance criteria:
- Perf: P95 token-latency for conversational workloads reduced by >= 7% on canary traffic compared to baseline; median latency unchanged or improved.
- Cost: token compute cost (estimated vGPU-seconds normalized) reduced by >= 4% for INT8-friendly workloads.
- Quality: zero significant regressions on guard evals (top-k sampling similarity, logprobs) — measured via existing evaluation harness.
- Observability: kernel provenance events show >= 95% coverage for canary traffic and feed into a dashboard.

Rollout plan:
1) Merge scheduler + harness behind a feature flag (config per-route).
2) Canary on low-volume customers and internal traffic, collect telemetry for 7 days.
3) Run simulation with collected traces to tune budget thresholds.
4) Gradual ramp to 20%/50%/100% with automatic rollback if P95 worsens > 5% or quality checks fail.

Testing & Validation:
- Unit tests for budget math and token accounting.
- Integration tests exercising prefill+decode dovetail where dequant epilogue overlaps next attention.
- Load tests with synthetic short-turn and long-context traces.
- A/B experiments with cost and quality metrics.

Telemetry & Dashboarding:
- Add provenance stream consumer that aggregates counts by kernel_variant, policy_decision, reason_code, and headwise breakdown.
- Dashboard widgets: tokenwise budget burn rate, kernel selection heatmap, delta-latency per kernel variant, and automatic anomaly alerts.

Risks & Mitigations:
- Risk: incorrect budgeting could cause more dequants, increasing cost. Mitigation: conservative default budgets and simulation tuning before ramp.
- Risk: telemetry overhead. Mitigation: provenance events must be compact (single-line protobuf) and sampled (configurable sampling rate) for high-volume routes.
- Risk: subtle quality regressions when preferring INT8 fastpath. Mitigation: automatic eval gating and canary quality checks.

Artifacts:
- Trace bundle (internal drive): https://drive.redwood/example/token-traces-q1-2026
- Design doc (Confluence): https://confluence.redwood/display/RUNTIME/Tokenwise+Requant+Budgeter
- Perf sim (repo): github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/perf-sim/pull/42
- Initial perf dashboard: https://grafana.redwood/internal/d/quant-budgeter-canary

Follow-ups:
- Policy auto-tuner that uses provenance events to do closed-loop threshold optimization.
- Headwise microkernel experimentation to enable mixed head precision within a single attention pass.

title: Cycle Pulse & Echo: Capacity Canvas and Slate Shepherding
preview: Problem: In high-velocity cycles we repeatedly hit two friction points: (1) teams over-commit early and then scramble to de-scope mid-cycle, and (2) platform ops can't reliably forecast burst capacity needs for Dedicated on short notice. Scope: establish a repeatable lightweight process and artifacts (Capacity Canvas + Slate Shepherd) that combine quantitative capacity budgeting with qualitative tradeoff surfaces so PMs and Eng leads can make deterministic cycle choices. Goals: reduce mid-cycle scope churn by 30%, shorten decision turnaround for staffing reallocations to <48h, and provide a 2-week predictive window for Dedicated capacity requests. Approach: introduce "Pulse" checkpoints (pre-cycle, week2 pulse, mid-cycle pulse) with clear signals and an "Echo" reserve policy (micro-credits pool + shadow roster). Deliverables: (1) Capacity Canvas template (workload-weighted token-hours, critical-path roles, cross-team friction points), (2) Slate Shepherding rubric (decomposition rules, soft/hard deltas, canary gating), (3) Runbook for triggering micro-credit allocations and emergency de-scope flows, (4) Dashboard cards and alert thresholds in Console to show capacity burn and reserve projections. Tradeoffs: simpler heuristics reduce cognitive load but may underfit highly variable infra bursts; we include a small modelling component to flag low-confidence forecasts. Stakeholders: PMs, Eng leads, SRE, People Ops for short-term staffing, Solutions for Dedicated customer communications. Success metrics: mid-cycle scope changes reduced; average time-to-allocate staff from pool; Dedicated capacity requests predicted 2 weeks ahead with >75% accuracy. This ticket covers articulation of templates, decision criteria, small pilot with two initiatives, and tooling hooks for alerts. Links to artifacts and PRs included below.

title: Align typography, spacing, and color ramps for charts and tables
preview:
Purpose: Reduce visual drift across data-heavy components (tables, charts, cards) by consolidating color ramps, tightening the typography scale at small sizes, and standardizing spacing and corner radii. Motivation: recent QA and customer feedback called out inconsistent row padding, low-contrast legends in charts, and varied shadow strength between card components. This ticket scopes token changes (color, type, spacing, radius, elevation), the compatibility strategy, and delivery plan for the March DS release.

Proposed changes (high level):
- Color: introduce a unified neutral ramp for data surfaces (neutral-050..neutral-900) and a separate semantic ramp for data accents (accent-050..accent-700). Increase contrast for legend/text-on-data to meet AA for 12px minimum sizes. Update primary/secondary swatches used in charts to reduce vibrancy clashes.
- Typography: tighten small-size scale: set body-xs 12/16, caption 11/14, table-cell 12/16; reduce line-height variance to improve row alignment. Document token names (type-xs, type-sm, type-base).
- Spacing/radius: standardize table row vertical padding to spacing-2 (8px) and spacing-3 (12px) as toggles; unify card radius to radius-2 (8px) for medium surfaces, radius-1 (4px) for compact controls.
- Shadow/elevation: reduce shadow spread for floating data cards to a subtle elevation (e-1), reserve e-2 for modal stacks; provide a compact shadow token set optimized for 1px device scale to avoid overly soft shadows on small cards.

Acceptance criteria:
- Tokens published to the design tokens repo with clear migration docs and code examples for React components.
- Charts and table components updated in the component library storybook demonstrating before/after visuals.
- Legend and table text meet WCAG AA for minimum 12px sizes in both dark and light themes.
- Frontend integration PRs created with feature flags for a canary rollout.

Rollout plan:
1) Publish token file and mapping guide.
2) Swap tokens in isolated chart + table stories and run visual diffs.
3) Create frontend PR to toggle tokens behind a runtime flag for 10% of users (canary).
4) Collect metrics (error/visual regressions/feedback) for 2 weeks, iterate.
5) Full rollout in design-system-1.9 if no regressions.

Notes on migration: Avoid renaming core tokens used widely (like spacing-1..4). Instead introduce alias tokens (eg. data-row-padding -> spacing-2) and deprecate old names with a 2-release window. Work with ENG to add a compatibility layer in token loader so older releases can map automatically.

Links and artifacts:
- Figma board: https://www.figma.com/file/REDACTED/data-tokens
- Storybook mocks (internal): https://storybook.redwood.ai/ds/data-tokens
- Draft PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/design-tokens/pull/124

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Small text in dense tables may still fail contrast if customers use custom theme overrides. Mitigation: add linting checks to CI and doc guidance for theme authors.
- Risk: Shadow changes may look flatter on high-DPI devices. Mitigation: include device-specific token suggestions and test on macOS Retina and common Android devices.

Increase contrast for legend/text-on-data to meet AA for 12px minimum sizes. Update primary/secondary swatches used in charts to reduce vibrancy clashes.
- Typography: tighten small-size scale: set body-xs 12/16, caption 11/14, table-cell 12/16; reduce line-height variance to improve row alignment. Document token names (type-xs, type-sm, type-base).
- Spacing/radius: standardize table row vertical padding to spacing-2 (8px) and spacing-3 (12px) as toggles; unify card radius to radius-2 (8px) for medium surfaces, radius-1 (4px) for compact controls.
- Shadow/elevation: reduce shadow spread for floating data cards to a subtle elevation (e-1), reserve e-2 for modal stacks; provide a compact shadow token set optimized for 1px device scale to avoid overly soft shadows on small cards.

Acceptance criteria:
- Tokens published to the design tokens repo with clear migration docs and code examples for React components.
- Charts and table components updated in the component library storybook demonstrating before/after visuals.
- Legend and table text meet WCAG AA for minimum 12px sizes in both dark and light themes.
- Frontend integration PRs created with feature flags for a canary rollout.

Rollout plan:
1) Publish token file and mapping guide.
2) Swap tokens in isolated chart + table stories and run visual diffs.
3) Create frontend PR to toggle tokens behind a runtime flag for 10% of users (canary).
4) Collect metrics (error/visual regressions/feedback) for 2 weeks, iterate.
5) Full rollout in design-system-1.9 if no regressions.

Notes on migration: Avoid renaming core tokens used widely (like spacing-1..4). Instead introduce alias tokens (eg. data-row-padding -> spacing-2) and deprecate old names with a 2-release window. Work with ENG to add a compatibility layer in token loader so older releases can map automatically.

Links and artifacts:
- Figma board: https://www.figma.com/file/REDACTED/data-tokens
- Storybook mocks (internal): https://storybook.redwood.ai/ds/data-tokens
- Draft PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/design-tokens/pull/124

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: Small text in dense tables may still fail contrast if customers use custom theme overrides. Mitigation: add linting checks to CI and doc guidance for theme authors.
- Risk: Shadow changes may look flatter on high-DPI devices. Mitigation: include device-specific token suggestions and test on macOS Retina and common Android devices.

title: LandingPad sprint allocator and release-train governance
preview:
Problem statement: As multiple feature teams target overlapping launch windows across runtime, console, and API surfaces, we lack a single, enforceable runway allocator that ties launch dates to capacity reservations, OKR deadlines, and rollback-safe guardrails. This creates last-minute contention for Dedicated capacity, elevated latency risk, and misaligned communications for enterprise customers.

Goal: Build the "LandingPad" sprint allocator — a lightweight governance pattern and supporting coordination checklist that: (1) maps launches to capacity lanes (baseline, hedge, emergency), (2) enforces gating criteria per surface (runtime/console/api), (3) publishes a one-page cross-team runway with rollout owners and rollback triggers, and (4) integrates with quarterly OKRs and the release calendar so product planning and platform capacity are aligned.

Scope and deliverables:
- Define LandingPad governance doc (roles, lanes, gating matrix).
- Create a templated runway artifact in Confluence that PMs can claim per feature and which auto-summarizes capacity needs.
- Lightweight signals & thresholds for autoscaling/capacity reservations (input to ENG tickets ENG-9841/ENG-12012).
- A Slack release-train channel template and weekly cadence (owner rotation) for cross-surface go/no-go.
- Run a pilot with two teams (runtime model-quantization rollout and console reporting migration) during the 2026.04 train.

Non-goals: We will not build a full scheduling automation engine in this ticket — this is governance + templates + two pilots. Full automation will be a follow-up if pilots show >30% reduction in contention events.

title: Compact aggregate announcer + render-decimation for high-density time-series
preview:
Problem:
High-density time-series views (dashboards with many small series or very long histories) are causing two user-facing issues: 1) screen-reader consumers get overwhelmed by thousands of live announcements when the chart updates, and 2) interactive latency spikes (hover/tooltip + legend updates) as DOM nodes and per-series layers multiply.

Goal:
Design a lightweight, accessible pattern and visual affordance that: 1) collapses non-essential series into an ARIA-friendly summary that screen readers can consume without noise, 2) preserves discoverability and keyboard navigation for power users, and 3) reduces DOM and paint overhead to improve interactive latency for common dashboard workloads.

Constraints:
- Must be implementable without breaking existing SDK consumers of chart components.
- Must respect tokenized color and spacing system (so theme changes cascade).
- Should provide clear fallback for low-density mode (no change to current experience).
- Must meet Redwood console accessibility baseline and SLOs for interaction latency.

Proposed solution (high level):
1) Compact Aggregate Announcer: an opt-in chart mode that groups low-importance series into a single "aggregate" cluster. For assistive tech we expose a single ARIA region summarizing the hidden series (count, min/max/last value, top-changed series) and an explicit "expand" control that keyboard + SR users can trigger to enumerate items on demand.
2) Render-decimation pipeline: introduce a rendering pre-pass that computes a tile-level density map and decides per-tile rendering strategy: full vector for focused tiles, decimated canvas raster for background tiles, and a static image snapshot for extremely dense/idle tiles. This reduces per-series repaint and DOM churn.
3) Legend & tooltip strategy: replace per-series legend DOM nodes with a virtualized legend list. Tooltips for hidden/aggregated series are surfaced in the aggregate summary panel rather than as individual popovers.
4) Token alignment: add two new design tokens (chart.aggregation-badge-color, chart.aggregate-announcer-font-size) and update the compact legend token set to ensure visual parity between expanded and collapsed states.

Trade-offs considered:
- Hiding series by default reduces discoverability for novice users. Mitigation: show visually prominent soft-hint ("N collapsed") with one-click expand, and keep expand control in keyboard tab order.
- Rasterization sacrifices crispness for performance on extreme zooms. Mitigation: prefer vector for focused tiles and only rasterize background tiles; provide device-pixel-ratio-aware snapshots.

Success metrics / acceptance criteria:
- Screen reader tests: SR walkthrough of a 200+ series chart yields a single concise summary read by NVDA/VoiceOver by default, with an expand action that reads per-series items on demand.
- Performance: for a synthetic dashboard with 5K series (resampled to display resolution), interactive hover latency (time between mousemove and visible hover highlight update) improves by >= 40% compared to baseline.
- DOM nodes: per-chart DOM nodes reduced by >= 70% for high-density views.
- Visual parity: default view for under-20-series charts is identical to current UX.

Deliverables:
- Design mockups (compact + expanded states) and microinteraction spec (enter/exit aggregate, keyboard flows).
- New token suggestions and token migration notes for design system owner.
- Prototype: interactive Figma with overlay annotations + small React prototype integrating virtualized legend list.
- Accessibility test plan and scripts for manual SR testing.

Acceptance checklist:
- [ ] Mockups reviewed and approved by PM (Lina Gomez) and Engineering lead (Mateo Ruiz).
- [ ] Token PR submitted to design-tokens repo with fallback tokens.
- [ ] Prototype PR with smoke perf measurements attached.
- [ ] Usability session with 4 participants including 1 screen-reader user scheduled and completed.

Notes on related work:
- This ticket intersects with ENG-21012 (rendering pipeline refactor) and PM-502 (dashboard aggregation UX). Coordinate pre-deploy contract with runtime team to avoid double work.

title: Attention kernel choosing safety-net and synthetic benchmark simulator
preview:
Problem: We continue to see intermittent latency regressions and memory spikes when the kernel selector flips between FlashAttention v2 and v3 at certain batch/sequence-shape combinations during prefill and decode. Root cause is unclear across cases: some relate to v3 kernel workspace fragmentation, others to startup jitter when speculative warmup probes run.

Goal: Build a synthetic benchmark + simulator that can: 1) reliably reproduce the shape/occupancy conditions that trigger regressions, 2) exercise the kernel selection logic (including hysteresis, gating, and fallback), and 3) produce a set of safety thresholds and automatic rollback triggers for staged rollouts. The simulator should be lightweight, deterministic for CI, and capable of running on CI/GHA runners and on an engineering GPU pool.

Scope: implement workload generator, kernel selector harness (pluggable for v2/v3/quantized variants), telemetry exporters (latency, mem footprint, workspace alloc/reuse stats, kernel switch frequency), regression detector, and an evaluation dashboard export. Deliverables include a reproducible harness, a README with experiment recipes, an initial set of safety thresholds, and a PR with a canary rollout plan tied to runtime-1.19.

title: Dynamic SLI onboarding and traffic escrow policy for prioritized conversational endpoints
preview:
Background: As we onboard more conversational routes and tuned model variants, teams need a predictable way to declare SLIs and safely migrate traffic without risking noisy error-budget burn across regions. This ticket defines a prescriptive onboarding flow that (1) creates per-model SLIs from sampled traffic and synthetic probe signals, (2) allocates an "traffic escrow" (error-budget currency) to new model variants, and (3) wires an automated regional failover choreography tied to the escrow balance and circuit-breaker state. The goal is to reduce rollouts that unexpectedly exhaust global error budgets and to enable short-lived experiments with automatic safe ejection.

Scope:
- Define canonical SLI signals for conversational endpoints (latency P95/P99, request-quality pass rate, token-error-rate, downstream HTTP error rate).
- Specify sampling rates and synthetic probe cadence for initial SLI estimation.
- Design the traffic escrow model: how much traffic/time is allocated to a variant, how escrow is consumed and replenished, and how credits are reconciled across regions.
- Define failover choreography triggered by escrow depletion, circuit open events, or regional capacity pressure (including warm-standby behaviors).
- Produce operational playbook snippets for oncall: what alerts mean, automated remediation steps, and manual escalation checklist.

Out of scope: long-term SLI visualization UI improvements (console work will be a follow-up), and per-tenant economic billing changes.

title: Tenant-aware fairness simulator for regional scarcity and graceful degradation
preview: Goal: validate that admission control, circuit breakers, and load-shedding policies enforce predictable SLO degradation across tenants when a region experiences progressive GPU capacity loss or dependency silence. We need a reproducible simulator and harness that can: (1) emulate partial region failures (gradual GPU eviction, abrupt node loss, increasing enqueue delays), (2) emulate downstream dependency silence/muting (redis/cache/kv proxies returning timeouts or dropped responses without raising downstream alerts), and (3) measure per-tenant SLOs and fairness metrics under different admission/policy configurations. The output will inform runtime default policies and provide test cases for canary gating of autoscaling and admission improvements.

title: Token Spend Explorer — Role-aware Attribution & Pricing Presets
preview:
Goal: Design a Token Spend Explorer UX that gives billing owners and engineers a single-pane view to understand token-level spend across models, routes, and users, and to export cost-attribution results. The view must support role-aware defaults (Billing Admin vs Engineering Lead), pricing presets (flat-rate, slabed-tiers, custom per-model overrides), and an export wizard that produces a reconciliable CSV for accounting.

Scope:
- Main explorer surface: filter bar (time range, model, route, project tag, user role), column composer (tokens, prompt tokens, completion tokens, cost, cost-per-1k, cache-savings estimate), and grouped breakdown cards (by route, by model, by tag).
- Attribution modes: direct token attribution (by request), proportional attribution (split across route components), and cohort-level allocation (by project or customer tag).
- Pricing presets: built-in templates (Hosted-default, Dedicated-commit, Enterprise-custom) plus UI to create & apply a custom pricing profile that affects computed cost previews.
- Export wizard: stepper that selects attribution mode, pricing profile, columns, and reconciliation keys (request_id, session_id). Output: CSV and downloadable JSON lines for audit ingestion.
- Role-aware UI: Billing Admin sees pricing presets and export columns by default; Engineer sees latency/cost tradeoffs and sample prompts usage in addition to cost.

Design constraints and non-goals:
- Does not implement live cost syncing to billing system; export must be reconciliable but ingestion is a separate integration task.
- Does not include per-token content inspection; only metadata-driven attribution.

UX goals:
- Fast insight: median cost-per-call and 95th percentile token spikes at top of page.
- Explainability: tooltip-friendly equations showing how cost derived from tokens + pricing profile.
- Confidence for finance: export includes checksums and a provenance header with model version, pricing profile id, and time window.

Acceptance criteria:
- Prototype demonstrates switching attribution modes and shows immediate cost recalculation.
- Export wizard produces a sample CSV (<= 100 rows) for reviewer validation.
- Role-aware defaults applied and visible in header.
- Accessibility: all interactive elements keyboard-focusable and color contrast meets AA.

Design artifacts and links: [Figma token spend explorer prototype] https://www.figma.com/file/XXXX/Token-Spend-Explorer, [Design brief] https://docs.redwood.ai/confluence/token-cost-explorer, [Sample export spec] https://drive.redwood.ai/exports/token_export_spec_v1.csv

title: Create compact core token set for embedded widgets and dense UIs
preview:
Summary:
Create a compact variant of Redwood's core design tokens to support embedded widgets and dense data surfaces (small charts, table rows, alert banners inside third-party apps). This work introduces a namespaced compact token set (e.g. token-compact/*) that maps to existing tokens via aliases, updates the type scale for small contexts, tightens spacing and radius tokens, and flattens shadow/elevation for low-profile surfaces.

Problem / motivation:
Embedded widgets often render within host UIs with constrained vertical space. Using the default token set causes visual bloat and inconsistent alignment with host apps. We need a formally supported compact token set so product teams can opt into denser layouts without bespoke overrides. This also reduces the number of one-off component overrides engineers maintain.

Scope (what's in):
- Define compact variants for color palette keys (secondary/neutral emphasis), compact type scale (small/label/body-sm), condensed spacing tokens (gap-2..gap-8), smaller radii tokens (radius-sm/radius-xs), and reduced elevation tokens (shadow-sm/none).
- Add token aliases so components can opt-in via a theme flag (theme: compact).
- Update core components used in embedded flows: Button (compact size), Table row density option, Chart axes label sizes, Form inputs (compact density).

Out of scope:
- Global restyling of all Redwood Console screens. Default theme remains unchanged.

Expected impact:
- Reduce vertical height of embedded widgets by ~18-28% depending on component composition.
- Consistent appearance across hosted/dedicated/private deployments when using embedded SDKs.
- Easier product adoption of compact footprint for partners and customers embedding Redwood widgets.

title: Compact Alert Composer Canvas — Guided Rules, Handoff Shortcuts, and Cost Footprint Ribbon
preview: Design a condensed Alert Composer canvas that surfaces three primary dimensions in a single, scannable strip: (1) guided rule creation (thresholds, noisy suppression heuristics), (2) on-call handoff quicklinks (postmortem templates, responder selection, runbook pinning), and (3) an inline cost footprint ribbon that estimates token/compute cost impact and suggests cost-savvy mitigation (aggregation window, sampling, dedup). The goal is to reduce time-to-alert creation for platform users, reduce noisy alerts by encouraging sensible defaults, and make cost tradeoffs explicit at creation time.

title: Pinned version I/O covenant and regression protocol
preview: Define and publish a machine-readable covenant for pinned model versions that guarantees I/O shape, parameter aliases, and a published performance signature. The covenant will be enforced by onboarding checks and the regression pipeline. This ticket scopes the contract format, tolerated deltas, validation harness, and the publishing flow to the model catalog. Goal: give product teams a clear SLA-like promise for pinned models that balances strictness (prevent regressions) with pragmatic adapters (parameter aliasing, safe canonicalizers).
